food_subgroup: Chilled / set milk-based and coconut-based puddings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for coconut pudding; 'pudding food product' (FOODON_03301710) is the closest parent class covering set dessert puddings.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers puddings and similar desserts as a category; no specific coconut pudding entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for coconut pudding as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of coconut pudding is approximately 150 g, equivalent to one individual chilled portion cup as typically served at a restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Coconut pudding / coconut milk dessert — estimated from USDA FoodData Central coconut milk (full-fat) and sugar components, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA coconut-based dessert analogues
nutrient_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for coconut pudding (with nata de coco) exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining: (1) USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170172 'Coconut milk, raw (liquid expressed from grated meat and water)' for the coconut milk base; (2) USDA FDC ID 169655 'Sugars, granulated' for added sugar; (3) nata de coco is predominantly water and carbohydrate with negligible fat and protein, modelled from USDA FDC ID 168537 'Coconut water' as a low-calorie aqueous component; (4) agar/gelatin as a minor gelling agent. The final composite reflects approximately 55–60% coconut milk by weight in the set pudding, diluted by nata de coco and water. Values are cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA coconut-based dessert analogues. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of coconut milk-based desserts — analogous estimate based on 'Coconut milk' and 'milk-based pudding' GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for coconut pudding with nata de coco. GI is estimated at 40 (low) based on: (1) coconut milk has a low GI (~40) due to high fat content slowing gastric emptying; (2) milk-based puddings typically have GI 35–50 per University of Sydney GI Database; (3) nata de coco is a fermented coconut gel with negligible glycemic impact; (4) added sugar raises GI slightly but fat content moderates the overall response. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (40 × 13.0 g carbohydrate per 100g × 1.5) / 100 = 7.8 g, classified as low GL. Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~18 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Coconut pudding is low in sodium (~27 mg per 150 g serving), making it acceptable for individuals with hypertension when consumed in moderation, though the high saturated fat content warrants attention in the context of overall cardiovascular risk management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content (~11.5 g per 100 g) and added sugar from sweetened coconut milk base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Coconut pudding contains approximately 17 g of sugar per 150 g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size and account for this dessert within their daily carbohydrate budget, noting that the low GI (~40) and high fat content moderate the postprandial glucose response compared to high-GI desserts.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat content (~7.5 g per 100 g) from coconut milk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Coconut pudding is rich in saturated fat (~11.3 g per 150 g serving) primarily from coconut milk medium-chain triglycerides; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dessert occasionally and in small portions, as high saturated fat intake is associated with elevated LDL cholesterol and increased cardiovascular risk per current dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Coconut Pudding (椰香布丁, Yē xiāng bùdīng) is a chilled Chinese-style dessert made primarily from full-fat coconut milk set into a smooth, silky pudding using agar or gelatin. The dish is enriched with nata de coco — small, translucent, chewy cubes of fermented coconut gel — which provide a contrasting texture to the creamy pudding base. It is finished with toasted coconut shavings on top, adding a nutty aroma and a light crunch. The flavour profile is rich, mildly sweet, and distinctly coconut-forward, with a cool, refreshing quality that makes it a popular dessert in Singaporean Chinese restaurants and Taiwanese-origin chains such as Din Tai Fung. Nutritionally, coconut pudding is moderate in calories (~130 kcal per 100 g) and high in saturated fat from coconut milk (~7.5 g per 100 g), with moderate sugar (~11.5 g per 100 g) and low sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), meaning it produces a relatively moderate postprandial blood glucose response compared to high-sugar desserts. Clinically, the high saturated fat content is a consideration for individuals managing cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia, while the moderate sugar content requires portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally dairy-free, gluten-free, and halal-suitable, making it accessible to a wide range of dietary preferences. It is commonly served as a dessert course in Chinese dim sum restaurants and Taiwanese-style dining establishments across Singapore and Southeast Asia.
common_user_queries: How many calories in coconut pudding?
Is coconut pudding suitable for diabetics?
Din Tai Fung coconut pudding nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for coconut pudding with nata de coco exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA as a composite dish. Values are estimated from component-level data using USDA FoodData Central coconut milk and sugar entries, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA coconut dessert analogues. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database coconut milk and milk pudding data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are approved and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level approved sources (USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 170172 for coconut milk, FDC ID 169655 for sugar) as no exact composite entry exists for this dish. Values should be updated if HPB Singapore Foodo or a direct laboratory analysis of this specific product becomes available. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as coconut milk and plant-based ingredients contain no industrially produced trans fats; naturally zero by ingredient composition. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as coconut milk is a plant-derived ingredient containing no dietary cholesterol; naturally zero. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources used for estimation. 5. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources. 6. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources. 7. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources. 8. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources. 9. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as this is a plant-based dessert with no animal-derived ingredients; not reported in component sources. 10. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources. 11. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources; coconut milk is not a significant source of vitamin D. 12. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reliably reported in the component sources. 13. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources. 14. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on analogous coconut milk and milk pudding data from University of Sydney GI Database; direct measurement for this specific product is not available. 15. Vegan status is marked false pending confirmation of whether gelatin (animal-derived) or agar (plant-derived) is used as the setting agent in the Din Tai Fung formulation; if agar is confirmed, the dish would be vegan. 16. FoodOn match FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 code A0CKW are closest_parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for coconut pudding with nata de coco.
